h1Title: Aniseikonia (Unequal Perceived Image Size Between Eyes)
heroOverview: Aniseikonia is a binocular vision condition in which the perceived size or shape of the image formed by one eye differs meaningfully from that formed by the other, preventing or disrupting comfortable binocular fusion. The word is derived from Greek: an (without), iso (equal), eikon (image). A size difference of 0.5–1% between the two retinal images is within normal tolerance; differences of 1–5% produce noticeable visual discomfort; differences above 5% typically cause frank diplopia or loss of binocular vision. Prevalence data are limited but clinical estimates suggest approximately 1–2% of the population have clinically symptomatic aniseikonia, though the condition is substantially under-recognised because its symptoms closely overlap with eyestrain, migraine, and vestibular disorders. Aniseikonia is most commonly caused by anisometropia — unequal refractive errors between the eyes — corrected with spectacle lenses, and by retinal disease that physically distorts or stretches the photoreceptors. Accurate diagnosis requires specialised eikonometry or aniseikonia testing software, and management with iseikonic or size lenses is highly effective when the correct inter-eye size difference is identified and prescribed.

keyStats.prevalence: Clinically significant aniseikonia estimated in approximately 1–2% of the population; substantially under-diagnosed due to symptom overlap with headache and vestibular disorders
keyStats.demographics: Most common in patients with anisometropia ≥2–3 D corrected with spectacles, and in patients after unilateral retinal surgery or cataract extraction
keyStats.avgAge: Typically identified in adults aged 20–70; more symptomatic presentations after 40 as fusional reserves decline with age
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated several tens of millions affected worldwide when sub-threshold symptomatic cases are included; exact prevalence studies are lacking

definition: Aniseikonia (ICD-10: H52.32) is a condition in which the ocular images perceived by the two eyes differ in size or shape, disrupting the brain's ability to fuse them into a single comfortable binocular percept. The condition falls under disorders of refraction and accommodation and is managed by ophthalmologists and optometrists with subspecialty expertise in binocular vision. It is classified as static aniseikonia (constant size difference) or dynamic aniseikonia (size difference that changes with gaze direction, more common with retinal disease). Further, the image size difference may be isotropic — equal in all meridians (overall magnification difference) — or meridional — confined to one meridian (causing one image to appear wider or taller than the other). Isotropic aniseikonia most commonly results from optical magnification differences produced by spectacle lenses of different power correcting anisometropia. Meridional aniseikonia is typically produced by asymmetric astigmatic corrections. Induced aniseikonia from spectacle lenses is calculated by the lens form equation: the magnification of a spectacle lens increases with thicker lens design, steeper front surface curves, and greater vertex distance from the eye — the variables manipulated in iseikonic lens design to reduce the size difference. Retinal aniseikonia arises when physical changes to the retinal architecture — macular pucker, epiretinal membrane, macular oedema, or surgical retinopexy — alter the effective spacing of photoreceptors in one eye, changing the perceived image size or shape independent of any optical difference. Patients with aniseikonia report spatial distortions, headaches, eyestrain, nausea, and diplopia, with symptoms often most pronounced during reading and activities requiring precise spatial judgement.

typesClassification.description: Caused by spectacle lens design when different powers are prescribed for each eye in anisometropia; the dominant and most common form, occurring whenever a spherical difference of 2–3 D or more is corrected with spectacle lenses.

Caused by physical changes to the retina — such as epiretinal membrane, macular pucker, or retinal detachment with reattachment — that distort or compress photoreceptors in one eye, altering perceived image size independent of any refractive correction.
The image in one eye appears uniformly larger or smaller in all directions (overall magnification difference); most commonly from spherical anisometropia corrected with spectacle lenses.
The image difference is confined to one axis — one image appears wider but not taller, or taller but not wider — typically from cylindrical (astigmatic) lens power differences between the two eyes.

The size difference changes with gaze direction; more common with retinal aniseikonia where the distortion varies across the retinal surface, and with high-powered spectacle lenses where magnification varies in different areas of the lens.

primarySymptoms: Spatial distortion — objects, particularly vertical lines or edges, appear to lean, tilt, or curve differently in each eye, producing a 3D world that looks geometrically skewed or uncomfortable.
Headaches, typically frontal or brow-centred, that develop during binocular near tasks and reflect the sustained neural effort required to reconcile two images of different sizes.
Eyestrain and eye fatigue during reading or screen work, usually emerging within minutes of beginning a binocular near task rather than building over hours as in accommodative fatigue.
Nausea or a vague sense of motion sickness during visual tasks, particularly when moving through complex environments, derived from the spatial conflict produced by unequal image sizes.
Binocular diplopia — double vision from failure of fusion — which may be intermittent and is typically preceded by other asthenopic symptoms as the size disparity overwhelms fusional reserves.
Difficulty with depth perception in familiar environments — doors, steps, or stairs may appear displaced or the spatial layout may seem unreliable.
A tendency to close one eye, turn the head, or adopt a head tilt to reduce the size discrepancy between the two eyes during near tasks.
Disorientation or loss of balance in complex spatial environments such as escalators, crowds, or patterned floors.
Post-operative visual disturbance after cataract surgery, macular surgery, or epiretinal membrane peeling, when the procedure alters one retina's photoreceptor spacing.
Difficulty tolerating a new spectacle prescription for corrected anisometropia, where adapting to the new aniseikonia takes weeks or does not occur.
earlyWarningSigns: Noticing that one eye sees objects as slightly larger or smaller than the other when tested separately with one eye covered.
Objects appearing to lean or tilt when viewed binocularly, even though the eyes are aligned and vision is clear in each eye separately.
Increasing intolerance to a previously well-tolerated spectacle prescription, particularly if the frame vertex distance or lens design has changed.
New spatial distortion following intraocular surgery or retinal laser treatment in one eye.
emergencySigns: Sudden onset of macropsia or micropsia (objects suddenly appearing much larger or smaller) in one eye, which can indicate a rapidly progressing macular lesion, central retinal vein or artery occlusion, or retinal detachment requiring same-day retinal assessment.
Aniseikonia accompanied by sudden photopsia (flashes of light) or new floaters, which raise immediate concern for a retinal tear or detachment.

Post-surgical monocular metamorphopsia (distorted vision) after cataract or vitreoretinal surgery that worsens rather than improves in the first week — requires prompt review by the operating surgeon.

causes.description: Spectacle lenses of different powers produce different optical magnifications: plus (hyperopic-correcting) lenses magnify, minus (myopic-correcting) lenses minify, and the greater the power difference between the two lenses, the larger the inter-eye image size difference. A 1 D spherical power difference creates approximately 1–2% aniseikonia with spectacles, so a 3 D inter-eye difference produces 3–6% — the threshold of clinical significance.

A fibrous membrane growing on the inner retinal surface can wrinkle or pucker the macula of one eye, compressing photoreceptors and increasing their effective density — causing that eye to perceive images as larger in the affected meridians (macropsia). This is the most common cause of structural retinal aniseikonia.

Scleral buckling procedures physically indent the eye wall, altering axial length and retinal topography; pneumatic retinopexy and vitrectomy change the retinal architecture around the area of detachment. Any of these can alter the apparent image size in the operated eye by several percent.

Conditions that produce macular oedema — diabetic macular oedema, central serous chorioretinopathy, age-related macular degeneration — can displace photoreceptors, altering perceived image size in the affected eye dynamically as oedema fluctuates.

Removing a cataract lens and implanting an intraocular lens of the wrong power creates a refractive anisometropia, and therefore aniseikonia. Aphakia — the absence of any intraocular lens — produces approximately 24–30% magnification with a spectacle lens, causing extreme aniseikonia that prevents binocular vision without a contact lens.

causes.cause: Anisometropia corrected with asymmetric contact lenses or lenses at different vertex distances
causes.description: Changes in the fitting of contact lenses — different vertex distances, base curves, or power compensations — alter the effective magnification at the eye's nodal point. Even small fitting changes in high anisometropia can shift aniseikonia across the clinical threshold.

causes.cause: Corneal or lenticular refractive surgery outcomes
causes.description: LASIK, PRK, or lens replacement that achieves unequal outcomes between the eyes creates an induced optical aniseikonia; planned bilateral surgery that differs in effect by more than 1–2 D can be symptomatic and may require further treatment.

riskFactors.factor: High anisometropia corrected with spectacle lenses
riskFactors.description: The strongest risk factor for optical aniseikonia; a spectacle-corrected difference of 3 D or more between the eyes creates an image size difference of 3–6%, above which most patients develop symptoms. Switching to contact lenses reduces the magnification difference by approximately 60–80%.

Affects approximately 7–11% of adults over 65; unilateral membranes cause the affected eye to perceive a larger or distorted image, producing structural aniseikonia that cannot be corrected with lenses and may require surgical peeling.

Approximately 20–40% of patients after scleral buckling develop clinically significant aniseikonia from the induced axial length change and retinal topography alteration; the risk is higher with larger buckles closer to the posterior pole.

riskFactors.factor: Unilateral cataract surgery without IOL power matching
riskFactors.description: Accurate IOL power selection minimises post-operative anisometropia; residual anisometropia after surgery is a leading cause of aniseikonia in adults over 60. Simultaneous bilateral cataract surgery or targeting matched refractions reduces this risk.

riskFactors.factor: Central serous chorioretinopathy or diabetic macular oedema
riskFactors.description: Both conditions produce subretinal or intraretinal fluid that displaces photoreceptors, creating dynamic aniseikonia; treatment of the underlying condition (laser, anti-VEGF therapy, mineralocorticoid antagonists) typically resolves the aniseikonia as the fluid reabsorbs.

Older adults with marginal aniseikonia that was previously tolerated may develop symptoms as fusional vergence amplitudes decline with age, making previously sub-threshold image size differences clinically apparent.

riskFactors.factor: Change in spectacle vertex distance or frame design
riskFactors.description: Even modest changes in the distance from the spectacle lens to the cornea alter the effective magnification; patients with high prescriptions may develop new aniseikonia symptoms after a change of frame, even without any prescription change.

affectedDemographics: Adults with anisometropia ≥2.5–3 D corrected with spectacle lenses
Patients over 50 following unilateral cataract surgery with residual anisometropia
Patients at any age after scleral buckling or vitrectomy for retinal detachment
Adults over 65 with epiretinal membrane in one eye
Patients with central serous chorioretinopathy, predominantly men aged 30–55
Patients after asymmetric refractive surgery outcomes (LASIK, PRK)
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosing aniseikonia requires both identifying the symptom complex and quantifying the inter-eye image size difference. A comprehensive binocular vision history — particularly the presence of spatial distortion, image tilt, nausea, and asthenopia — provides the clinical suspicion. The New Aniseikonia Test (NAT) is the most widely available clinical tool: the patient, wearing anaglyph (red-green) glasses, adjusts two semicircles of different colours (one seen by each eye) until they appear the same size; the difference in the final setting quantifies the percentage aniseikonia in the tested meridians. The Remole Stereo Aniseikonia Test (RSAT) is an alternative computerised approach. For cases where retinal aniseikonia is suspected, Amsler grid testing, microperimetry, and OCT of the macula identify the structural cause. Calculated optical aniseikonia from spectacle prescriptions can be estimated using the Knapp and lens magnification formulae, allowing pre-prescription planning to minimise image size differences. For complex cases — particularly mixed optical and retinal aniseikonia — specialist binocular vision assessment at a university-based low vision or strabismus clinic provides the most complete evaluation. Monocular visual acuity, corneal topography, and retinal examination are integral to distinguishing optical from structural aniseikonia.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies the inter-eye image size difference as a percentage in horizontal and vertical meridians by having the patient match sizes of differently coloured semicircles seen by each eye through anaglyph glasses.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You wear red-green glasses and adjust a slider or select image sizes until the two coloured shapes appear equal; the setting difference is recorded. The test takes 10–15 minutes and is performed at the testing distance relevant to symptoms (near or distance).

diagnosticTests.purpose: Estimates the expected aniseikonia produced by a proposed spectacle prescription based on lens power, design, thickness, and vertex distance; guides lens selection before manufacturing.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed by the optician or optometrist using prescription data and frame measurements; no examination of the patient is required for the calculation itself.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies structural causes of retinal aniseikonia including epiretinal membrane, macular pucker, oedema, and subretinal fluid that physically alter photoreceptor spacing.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A rapid 3–5 minute scan of the back of each eye; no discomfort, though dilation drops may be instilled for optimal image quality.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects macular distortion (metamorphopsia) caused by retinal aniseikonia; an abnormal Amsler grid in one eye with a normal fellow eye is a sensitive indicator of structural retinal disease as the aniseikonia source.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You cover one eye and look at a grid of small squares at arm's length; you report whether any lines appear wavy, missing, or differently sized than expected.

diagnosticTests.test: Corneal topography and anterior segment OCT
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies corneal irregularities or asymmetric astigmatism as contributors to meridional aniseikonia, guiding whether rigid contact lenses or iseikonic spectacle lenses are more appropriate.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Painless 2–3 minute imaging of the corneal shape; no drops required.

diagnosticTests.test: Space eikonometer or stereoscopic space perception test
diagnosticTests.purpose: A specialised test of perceived depth and spatial layout under binocular conditions, sensitive to small aniseikonia values that standard NAT testing may miss in complex cases.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You look at a stereoscopic scene and judge the depth positions of objects; anomalies in apparent depth sequence indicate aniseikonia even without overt diplopia.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of aniseikonia is directed at reducing the inter-eye image size difference to within the patient's fusional tolerance. For optical aniseikonia from anisometropic spectacle correction, three approaches are used: first, switching from spectacles to contact lenses, which sit at the corneal plane and reduce the image size difference by approximately 60–80% compared with equivalent spectacle lenses — the most immediately effective intervention for most patients. Second, iseikonic spectacle lens design adjusts lens thickness, front surface curvature (iseikonic curves), and vertex distance to selectively alter the magnification of one lens until the two eyes' image sizes are equalised; this requires specialist binocular vision assessment to quantify the aniseikonia accurately and a laboratory with iseikonic capability to manufacture the lenses. Third, prescribing only a partial anisometropic correction — leaving some blur in the more ametropic eye to limit the image size difference — is occasionally used in adults when binocular comfort is more important than full visual acuity in the eye with the larger error. For retinal aniseikonia from epiretinal membrane causing significant symptoms, surgical peeling (vitrectomy with membrane dissection) often resolves or substantially reduces the aniseikonia in 70–80% of patients as the macula relaxes and photoreceptors return toward normal spacing, though a residual image size difference may persist. For macular oedema causes, treating the underlying condition — anti-VEGF injection for diabetic macular oedema or age-related macular degeneration, and rest or laser for central serous chorioretinopathy — resolves the structural distortion and typically reverses the aniseikonia. Digital therapeutic options including virtual reality aniseikonia training are emerging but currently lack sufficient clinical trial evidence to be considered standard care.

medicalTreatments.name: Contact lens correction (in place of spectacles)
medicalTreatments.description: Fitting contact lenses matched to each eye's prescription instead of spectacle lenses reduces the aniseikonic magnification difference by approximately 60–80%, because contact lenses at the corneal plane produce far less inter-eye size difference than spectacle lenses 14 mm in front of the eye for equivalent prescription differences.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Resolves symptoms in approximately 60–80% of patients with optical aniseikonia from anisometropia when contact lenses replace spectacles; success depends on the degree of initial anisometropia and tolerance of lens wear.

medicalTreatments.name: Iseikonic (size) spectacle lenses
medicalTreatments.description: Specially designed spectacle lenses with modified front surface curves, centre thickness, and vertex distance precisely calibrated to match the magnification of both eyes; prescribed after accurate eikonometry measurement of the inter-eye size difference.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces symptomatic aniseikonia by the targeted percentage when correctly designed; clinical series report symptom resolution in approximately 70–85% of patients with optical aniseikonia when iseikonic lenses are accurately prescribed.

medicalTreatments.name: Anti-VEGF injection therapy (for retinal aniseikonia from macular oedema)
medicalTreatments.description: Intravitreal anti-VEGF agents (ranibizumab, bevacizumab, aflibercept) reduce subretinal and intraretinal fluid in diabetic macular oedema and neovascular age-related macular degeneration, resolving photoreceptor displacement and reversing the structural aniseikonia as fluid reabsorbs.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Macular oedema resolution rates of 30–40% on anti-VEGF monotherapy at 1 year in diabetic trials; aniseikonia typically improves proportionally as central retinal thickness normalises.

medicalTreatments.name: Partial anisometropic correction (deliberate under-correction)
medicalTreatments.description: Prescribing less than the full corrective difference between the eyes reduces the optical magnification difference at the expense of some visual acuity in the more ametropic eye; used when full correction cannot be tolerated and contact lenses are not suitable.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Provides binocular comfort in approximately 40–60% of patients when the tolerable residual blur in one eye is acceptable and the resultant aniseikonia falls below the clinical threshold.

surgicalOptions.name: Vitrectomy with epiretinal membrane peeling
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical removal of the fibrous membrane from the macular surface allows the puckered retina to flatten, restoring more normal photoreceptor spacing and reducing or resolving structural aniseikonia; performed under local anaesthesia as a day-case procedure.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Approximately 70–80% of patients report improvement in metamorphopsia and aniseikonia after membrane peeling; residual aniseikonia persists in approximately 20–30% from irreversible photoreceptor rearrangement.

surgicalOptions.name: IOL exchange or supplementary IOL implantation
surgicalOptions.description: When residual anisometropia after cataract surgery is the cause of aniseikonia, replacing the primary IOL or implanting a supplementary piggyback IOL to correct the residual power reduces the spectacle-corrected anisometropia and its resulting aniseikonia.
surgicalOptions.successRate: IOL exchange achieves target refraction within 0.50 D in approximately 75–80% of cases; clinical series report aniseikonia symptom improvement in the majority of treated patients.

alternativeTreatments.name: Occlusion of one lens (monocular fogging)
alternativeTreatments.description: Tinting or frosting one spectacle lens reduces visual input from the more symptomatic eye, eliminating diplopia; used as a temporary measure while iseikonic correction is being designed or when no other intervention is feasible.

alternativeTreatments.name: Base-in or base-out prism combined with iseikonic design
alternativeTreatments.description: When aniseikonia co-exists with a horizontal heterophoria, combined prism and size-lens correction addresses both simultaneously; requires specialist binocular vision assessment to prescribe accurately.

whySeeSpecialist: Aniseikonia is frequently misdiagnosed as migraine, vestibular disorder, or anxiety because its symptoms — spatial distortion, nausea, headaches, and visual discomfort — are non-specific. An optometrist with binocular vision subspecialty expertise, or an ophthalmologist with a low vision or strabismus interest, is required to perform eikonometry and calculate iseikonic corrections. Retinal causes require an ophthalmologist with vitreoretinal subspecialty expertise. Patients who have repeatedly failed to adapt to spectacle corrections for anisometropia, or who report intractable spatial distortion after ocular surgery, particularly benefit from specialist assessment for undiagnosed aniseikonia.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for an optometrist certified in binocular vision (Fellow of the College of Optometrists in Vision Development or equivalent) who can perform the New Aniseikonia Test and has access to a laboratory that manufactures iseikonic lenses. For retinal causes, a vitreoretinal surgeon with experience in epiretinal membrane peeling is appropriate. Ask the clinician whether they measure aniseikonia quantitatively — practitioners who rely solely on symptom reports without measurement typically under-treat this condition. For complex cases with both optical and retinal components, joint assessment by an optometrist and ophthalmologist provides the best outcomes.
preventionStrategies: Correct anisometropia with contact lenses in preference to spectacles when the inter-eye difference is 2.5 D or more, preventing optical aniseikonia from the outset.
At cataract surgery planning, target matched refractions between the two eyes — aiming for bilateral low myopia or emmetropia — to minimise post-operative anisometropia and aniseikonia.

Seek early treatment for conditions predisposing to retinal aniseikonia: diabetic macular oedema, central serous chorioretinopathy, and early epiretinal membranes before distortion becomes established.
Use frame vertex distance measurements and iseikonic lens calculations when prescribing for anisometropia ≥3 D in spectacle-wearing patients.
Report any new spatial distortion or perceived image size difference after ocular surgery promptly to allow early intervention.
lifestyleModifications: Use contact lenses rather than spectacles during activities where spatial accuracy matters — driving, sports, stairs — to reduce the image size disparity at those times.
Keep spectacle frame vertex distance consistent; even a few millimetres change with a new frame alters magnification in high prescriptions.
Allow several weeks for visual system adaptation when a new iseikonic prescription is fitted; some residual symptoms during adaptation are expected.
Avoid reading in unusual head positions — prone, lying on one side — as this can increase the effective aniseikonia by changing the gaze angle through a spectacle lens.

Report worsening spatial distortion or new metamorphopsia promptly — progressive retinal aniseikonia from epiretinal membrane or macular oedema can be treated before irreversible photoreceptor damage occurs.

recommended: A diet low in glycaemic index for patients with diabetic macular oedema — the leading retinal cause of aniseikonia in adults under 70
Lutein and zeaxanthin-rich foods (leafy greens, eggs) for macular health in patients with epiretinal membrane risk
Adequate omega-3 fatty acids for general retinal and ocular surface health
avoid: High-sodium and high-sugar foods that raise blood pressure and blood glucose, both associated with accelerated macular oedema and epiretinal membrane progression
Excessive caffeine and alcohol during periods of acute aniseikonia symptoms, as they can worsen vestibular sensitivity and amplify nausea

exerciseGuidelines: Regular moderate exercise is encouraged and does not adversely affect aniseikonia. Good cardiovascular fitness improves blood pressure and glycaemic control, which reduces the risk of retinal causes. Patients with active retinal aniseikonia from macular oedema or recent surgical treatment should avoid high-impact exercise until cleared by the treating ophthalmologist, as elevated intraocular pressure during strenuous activity may affect healing. No restrictions apply to patients with purely optical aniseikonia.
dailyManagement: Wear contact lenses as the primary optical correction whenever possible if anisometropia exceeds 2.5 D, switching to iseikonic spectacles only for situations where contact lenses cannot be worn.
Use the Amsler grid daily at home to monitor for changes in metamorphopsia pattern — worsening distortion should be reported promptly.
Keep a symptom diary of headache, nausea, and spatial distortion severity; share this at follow-up visits to track treatment response and guide prescription adjustment.
Maintain prescribed monitoring intervals for retinal causes — typically 4–8 weekly for active macular oedema treatment, and 6–12 monthly for stable epiretinal membrane.

Inform any new optometrist about a known aniseikonia diagnosis before any prescription change is made, as seemingly small modifications to high-powered lenses can have disproportionate effects on image size balance.
prognosis: The prognosis of aniseikonia depends on its cause and whether treatment is optimised. Optical aniseikonia from anisometropic spectacle correction has an excellent prognosis: switching to contact lenses or prescribing iseikonic lenses resolves or substantially reduces symptoms in 70–85% of patients. Retinal aniseikonia from epiretinal membrane that is surgically peeled improves in approximately 70–80% of patients, though residual aniseikonia persists in 20–30% due to irreversible photoreceptor rearrangement. Aniseikonia from macular oedema typically resolves when the oedema is treated. In cases where a large retinal aniseikonia cannot be corrected optically and surgery has not produced sufficient improvement, monocular occlusion or accepting monocular suppression may be the endpoint of management. Overall, most patients with symptomatic aniseikonia can achieve comfortable binocular vision with appropriate specialist management.
recoveryTimeline: Patients switching from spectacles to contact lenses for optical aniseikonia typically notice improvement within days to a week. Adaptation to iseikonic spectacle lenses takes 2–4 weeks. After epiretinal membrane peeling, the retina takes 3–6 months to fully flatten; aniseikonia improvement is assessed at the 3-month and 6-month post-operative visits. Anti-VEGF treatment for macular oedema can reduce aniseikonia within 4–6 weeks of the first injection as fluid resolves.
complications: Suppression of one eye — when aniseikonia is large and persistent, the brain may suppress the input from the more distorted eye to eliminate diplopia, reducing binocular function and stereopsis.

Persistent diplopia — in cases where aniseikonia cannot be corrected to within fusional tolerance and surgical or optical options are exhausted, constant diplopia may require permanent monocular occlusion.

Chronic headaches and nausea — under-treated aniseikonia is a recognised cause of chronic daily headache that is frequently misdiagnosed as migraine; chronic visual-vestibular mismatch also produces persistent nausea.

Falls in older adults — spatial distortion from aniseikonia impairs depth perception and step judgement; older patients with post-surgical aniseikonia have higher short-term fall rates in case series.
Quality of life impact — moderate-to-severe aniseikonia restricts reading, driving, computer use, and social activities, with significant occupational and functional disability when untreated.

supportResources.name: American Academy of Ophthalmology — Aniseikonia patient information
supportResources.description: Patient-facing overview of aniseikonia from the American Academy of Ophthalmology.

supportResources.name: COVD — College of Optometrists in Vision Development
supportResources.description: Professional organisation for binocular vision specialists; includes a practitioner finder for optometrists trained in iseikonic lens prescribing and aniseikonia testing.

supportResources.name: NEI — Epiretinal membrane patient information. National Eye Institute
supportResources.description: Information on epiretinal membrane — the most common structural cause of retinal aniseikonia — including surgical treatment options.

sources.title: Winn B et al. Factors affecting light-adapted pupil size in normal human subjects. Investigative Ophthalmology and Visual Science, 1994 — background binocular vision reference
Highman VN. Spectacle magnification and aniseikonia. British Journal of Ophthalmology, 1977
Awaya S, Sugawara M, Miyake S. Aniseikonia and the relationship to binocular visual function. Documenta Ophthalmologica, 1979
Odell NV et al. Development and validation of the revised aniseikonia test (RNAT). British Journal of Ophthalmology, 2013
Kooijman AC, Stellingwerf T, Cornelissen FW. Aniseikonia caused by macular pucker: compensation with iseikonic contact lenses. Optometry and Vision Science, 2004

confusedWithConditions.name: Anisometropia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Anisometropia is the structural refractive difference between the eyes measured in dioptres; aniseikonia is the perceptual consequence — unequal image sizes — that anisometropia produces. They often occur together but are measured and treated with different tools.

confusedWithConditions.name: Diplopia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Diplopia is a symptom — double vision — that can result from aniseikonia when the image size difference overwhelms fusional reserves, but diplopia has many other causes. Aniseikonia is specifically about the image size mismatch, not a general misalignment.

confusedWithConditions.name: Exophoria
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Exophoria is a latent horizontal alignment deviation corrected by fusion; aniseikonia is a size and shape mismatch between the images. Both cause asthenopia, but exophoria is identified by cover testing while aniseikonia requires eikonometry.

confusedWithConditions.name: Presbyopia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Presbyopia causes near blur equally in both eyes from loss of accommodation; aniseikonia causes spatial distortion and headaches from unequal image sizes. They frequently co-exist in older patients, complicating spectacle prescription.

relatedConditions.name: Anisometropia
relatedConditions.relevance: The primary optical cause of aniseikonia; the two conditions are closely linked and aniseikonia is essentially the perceptual manifestation of anisometropia when corrected with spectacle lenses.

relatedConditions.name: Exophoria
relatedConditions.relevance: Latent heterophoria can co-exist with aniseikonia, and both impair binocular fusion; combined aniseikonia and exophoria requires simultaneous correction of both the size difference and the vergence demand.

relatedConditions.name: Diplopia
relatedConditions.relevance: Aniseikonia is an important and often missed cause of binocular diplopia, particularly post-surgical; excluding aniseikonia should be part of the workup for any patient with unexplained diplopia after ocular surgery.

relatedConditions.name: Convergence insufficiency
relatedConditions.relevance: Aniseikonia and convergence insufficiency both produce asthenopia and near-vision complaints; distinguishing them requires different tests (eikonometry for aniseikonia vs. vergence amplitude testing for convergence insufficiency).

relatedConditions.name: Keratoconus
relatedConditions.relevance: Asymmetric keratoconus produces high anisometropia and therefore significant optical aniseikonia; keratoconic aniseikonia is better managed with rigid contact lenses than iseikonic spectacle lenses.

faqs.question: What is aniseikonia?
faqs.answer: Aniseikonia means the two eyes perceive images of different sizes or shapes. Normally, the brain tolerates very small differences (under 1%), but when the gap exceeds about 1–5%, it causes spatial distortion, headaches, eyestrain, and sometimes double vision. It is most commonly caused by different spectacle prescriptions between the eyes or by retinal disease in one eye.

faqs.question: What causes aniseikonia?
faqs.answer: The most common cause is optical: correcting unequal prescriptions (anisometropia) with spectacle lenses produces lenses of different powers that magnify images differently. A 1-dioptre difference creates roughly 1–2% aniseikonia in spectacles. Retinal causes include epiretinal membrane, macular oedema, and retinal detachment surgery — all of which physically alter photoreceptor spacing in one eye.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of aniseikonia?
faqs.answer: Symptoms include spatial distortion (objects appearing to lean or curve), frontal headaches that develop early during near tasks, eyestrain, nausea or a sense of motion sickness during visual tasks, and intermittent double vision. Symptoms are often worse during reading or screen work and may be attributed to migraine or vestibular disorders before the ocular cause is identified.

faqs.question: How is aniseikonia diagnosed?
faqs.answer: The New Aniseikonia Test (NAT) quantifies the inter-eye size difference by having the patient match coloured shapes seen by each eye through anaglyph glasses. For retinal causes, macular OCT and Amsler grid testing identify the structural source of distortion. The diagnosis requires a binocular vision specialist — it is not detectable on routine eye chart testing.

faqs.question: Can aniseikonia be treated with contact lenses?
faqs.answer: Yes, and this is usually the first line for optical aniseikonia. Contact lenses sit on the cornea rather than 14 mm in front of it, so the same prescription differences produce far less image size difference — roughly 60–80% less than with spectacle lenses. Most patients with anisometropia-driven aniseikonia experience rapid symptom relief when contact lenses replace spectacles.

faqs.question: What are iseikonic lenses?
faqs.answer: Iseikonic lenses are specially designed spectacle lenses in which the front curve, thickness, and vertex distance are precisely calculated to match the magnification of both eyes. After measuring the inter-eye size difference with the New Aniseikonia Test, a binocular vision specialist can prescribe lenses that equalise the image sizes while still correcting the refractive error.

faqs.question: Is aniseikonia the same as anisometropia?
faqs.answer: No. Anisometropia is the structural difference in prescription between the eyes, measured in dioptres. Aniseikonia is the perceptual consequence — the brain sees images of different sizes. Anisometropia causes optical aniseikonia when corrected with spectacles, but aniseikonia can also arise from retinal disease with no refractive difference at all.

faqs.question: Can aniseikonia cause headaches?
faqs.answer: Yes, and headaches are one of the most common presenting symptoms. The brain works continuously to fuse two images of different sizes, and this sustained neural effort produces brow and frontal headaches that characteristically appear within minutes of binocular near work rather than after hours. Identifying and treating aniseikonia often resolves headaches that have been labelled as tension headache or migraine.

faqs.question: Can aniseikonia cause nausea or dizziness?
faqs.answer: Yes. Unequal image sizes create a mismatch between visual and vestibular spatial information that the brain interprets as motion, producing nausea, dizziness, and disorientation — particularly in moving environments. This is a recognised but often missed cause of chronic visual-vestibular mismatch syndrome in specialist neuro-ophthalmology clinics.

faqs.question: Is aniseikonia after cataract surgery normal?
faqs.answer: Some degree of aniseikonia is common after unilateral cataract surgery if the remaining eye has a significantly different prescription. Modern IOL power calculations target matching the operated eye's refraction to the fellow eye to minimise this, but residual anisometropia of 1–2 D is common. Persistent spatial distortion after cataract surgery should be assessed for aniseikonia.

faqs.question: Can epiretinal membrane cause aniseikonia?
faqs.answer: Yes. An epiretinal membrane (macular pucker) is a fibrous layer on the retinal surface that crinkles the macula, compressing photoreceptors and making one eye perceive images as larger or distorted in the affected area. This structural (retinal) aniseikonia differs from optical aniseikonia and may require surgical membrane peeling if symptoms are significant.

faqs.question: What is the difference between optical and retinal aniseikonia?
faqs.answer: Optical aniseikonia is caused by spectacle lenses of different powers magnifying images differently; it is correctable with contact lenses or iseikonic spectacle design. Retinal aniseikonia is caused by physical changes in one retina's architecture — from epiretinal membrane, oedema, or surgery — that alter perceived image size regardless of optics. The two types require different investigations and treatments.

faqs.question: How much aniseikonia is normal?
faqs.answer: A size difference of up to 0.5–1% between the two eyes is within normal tolerance and causes no symptoms in most people. Differences of 1–5% produce noticeable discomfort in most individuals. Differences exceeding 5% typically prevent comfortable binocular fusion and cause frank diplopia. These thresholds vary somewhat with individual fusional reserves and age.

faqs.question: Can aniseikonia go away on its own?
faqs.answer: Optical aniseikonia from spectacle correction does not resolve spontaneously — it persists as long as the anisometropic spectacle lenses are worn. Retinal aniseikonia from macular oedema often resolves when the oedema is treated successfully. Epiretinal membrane aniseikonia does not resolve without surgery. Monocular suppression is the brain's natural adaptation, but it comes at the cost of binocular vision.

faqs.question: Is aniseikonia common?
faqs.answer: Clinically significant symptomatic aniseikonia is estimated to affect approximately 1–2% of the population, but it is substantially under-recognised because its symptoms — headaches, nausea, spatial distortion — overlap with many other conditions. Among patients with anisometropia of 3 D or more corrected with spectacles, symptomatic aniseikonia affects a substantial proportion.

faqs.question: Can children have aniseikonia?
faqs.answer: Children with anisometropia can develop aniseikonia when glasses are worn, but the visual system adapts more readily in childhood than in adults. The more urgent concern in children is amblyopia from the underlying anisometropia, not the aniseikonia itself. Adults newly presenting with high anisometropic spectacle correction are the group most commonly symptomatic.

faqs.question: What is meridional aniseikonia?
faqs.answer: Meridional aniseikonia means the image size difference is confined to one direction — for example, one image appears wider but not taller. It is typically caused by different amounts of astigmatism or cylinder correction between the two eyes. It can cause images to appear parallelogram-shaped rather than simply different in overall scale.

faqs.question: Does aniseikonia affect depth perception?
faqs.answer: Yes. Accurate stereoscopic depth perception requires the brain to compare precisely matched images from both eyes. Unequal image sizes distort this comparison, causing objects to appear at wrong depths, edges to appear angled, or floors and stairs to appear tilted. Correcting aniseikonia often substantially improves stereo depth judgement.

faqs.question: When should I see a specialist for aniseikonia?
faqs.answer: Seek a binocular vision specialist if you have persistent headaches, spatial distortion, or nausea during near work that does not improve with a standard spectacle or contact lens prescription — particularly if you have anisometropia. Any new spatial distortion after eye surgery (cataract, retinal) should be assessed within 2–4 weeks. New macropsia or micropsia in one eye requires urgent retinal examination.

faqs.question: Can refractive surgery cure aniseikonia?
faqs.answer: Laser refractive surgery that equalises the prescription between the two eyes eliminates the optical cause of aniseikonia. If the inter-eye refractive difference can be reduced to 1 D or less by LASIK or PRK, the resulting aniseikonia from spectacle correction (if still worn) falls within normal tolerance. Surgery does not address retinal aniseikonia from structural causes.

metaDescription: Aniseikonia causes spatial distortion, headaches and nausea from unequal image sizes between eyes. Learn causes, iseikonic lenses and surgery. Doctor-reviewed.
